The surgeon isolates the inferior mesenteric artery, carefully dissecting away the surrounding fibro-fatty tissue. The artery is freed, and the pedicle is isolated posteriorly. Attention is given to preserving nearby nerves, which are visible close to the dissection area.
The surgeon circumferentially isolates the vessel, applying hemolog clips to ligate and divide it, ensuring secure closure of the vessel.

The surgeon performs lateral dissection to isolate the branch of the inferior mesenteric vein, which is then clipped and divided.
The surgeon clips and divides a tributary of the inferior mesenteric vein, isolating it from surrounding tissue.
The surgeon continues the dissection downward following the division of the pedicle, focusing on the area below the previously transected structures.
The surgeon applies medium-large titanium clips to the inferior mesenteric vein, preparing it for division.
After completing the clipping, the surgical team shifts focus towards the pelvis to begin the total mesorectal excision.

The surgeon creates a purse string suture using two strands of proline, securing the tissue in preparation for further surgical steps.

The surgeon completes the anastomosis and loosens the circular stapler, indicating the end of the stapling process in the rectal area.
